title: Matched-Budget Evaluation of Weak-View Residuals for One-Run DP Auditing
description: This project is a deliberately incremental empirical test built on one-run black-box DP auditing. It asks whether repeated weak, label-preserving views add measurable privacy evidence beyond the single-view quantile-regression baseline of Liu et al. (2025) when every consumed query is counted, tuning uses only public or external data, and matched-budget curves are averaged over randomized audit-pool orderings.
motivation: The strongest recent final-model one-run auditor already uses quantile regression to calibrate a clean-view score, so the remaining defensible gap is narrow. The real open question is not whether repeated-query membership inference exists, but whether weak-view statistics still add incremental signal beyond that baseline under honest query accounting, public-only calibration, and order-robust matched-budget evaluation.
proposed_approach: For each candidate, query the DP model on the clean input plus K weak augmentations, residualize clean and derived multi-view statistics with separate quantile regressors fit only on a public calibration split, and combine the clean residual, worst-view residual, and an epsilon-tuned agreement residual into a fixed structured score. Evaluate this against the Liu et al. single-view quantile baseline and a query-matched weak-view mean-only control on the same finite audit pool, averaging empirical-epsilon-versus-query curves over multiple randomized audit-pool orderings.

related_work.title: Privacy Auditing with One (1) Training Run
related_work.how_it_differs: Provides the one-run neighboring-dataset protocol. The proposed study keeps that protocol fixed and changes only the candidate score and fairness protocol.

related_work.title: Auditing f-Differential Privacy in One Run
related_work.how_it_differs: Tightens one-run privacy conversion. The proposed work is complementary and aims only to strengthen the empirical distinguisher before conversion.

related_work.title: Enhancing One-run Privacy Auditing with Quantile Regression-Based Membership Inference
related_work.how_it_differs: This is the direct baseline. That paper studies calibrated single-view scoring; the proposed work asks whether repeated weak-view residuals still add signal beyond it under matched total query budgets.

related_work.title: Semi-Leak: Membership Inference Attacks Against Semi-supervised Learning
related_work.how_it_differs: Shows that augmentation-based repeated queries can improve membership inference, so multi-view querying itself is not novel. The proposed work is narrower: one-run DP auditing, empirical privacy evidence, and a matched-budget comparison against the Liu et al. quantile baseline.

related_work.title: Sampling Attacks: Amplification of Membership Inference Attacks by Repeated Queries
related_work.how_it_differs: Makes repeated queries central by amplifying label-only membership signal through sampling, but it does not study one-run DP auditing, empirical epsilon lower bounds, or fixed-audit-pool matched-budget evaluation.

related_work.title: Label-Only Membership Inference Attacks
related_work.how_it_differs: Uses many perturbation queries to estimate decision-boundary distance in a generic label-only attack setting. The proposed work instead studies final-model DP auditing with honest accounting of all queries and a stronger quantile-regression baseline.

related_work.title: You Only Query Once: An Efficient Label-Only Membership Inference Attack
related_work.how_it_differs: This paper exists because repeated-query label-only attacks are expensive, which is directly relevant to fair query accounting. The proposed work evaluates that query-cost issue inside one-run DP auditing rather than proposing a new label-only attack.

related_work.title: Sequentially Auditing Differential Privacy
related_work.how_it_differs: Already covers anytime-valid sequential auditing. The proposed paper uses sequential analysis only as an optional evaluation wrapper for query efficiency.

related_work.title: Optimizing Canaries for One-Run Privacy Auditing of DP-SGD
related_work.how_it_differs: Optimizes canary selection directly. The proposed method instead holds canary families simple and tests whether each candidate can be scored better at inference time.

hypothesis: After controlling for sample difficulty with the single-view quantile-regression approach of Liu et al. (2025), repeated weak-view queries can provide modest additional audit signal through worst-view and agreement residuals, and some of that gain can survive public-only, epsilon-tuned, matched-budget evaluation against actual consumed queries.
success_criteria: In a majority of the 4 dataset/privacy settings, the structured weak-view score is directionally better than the Liu et al. single-view quantile baseline after pooling the 3 seeds.
Some of the lift remains visible when empirical epsilon lower bounds are plotted against actual consumed queries Q and averaged over multiple randomized audit-pool orderings.

The structured weak-view score usually matches or modestly exceeds a query-matched weak-view mean-only control, indicating value from worst-view or agreement statistics rather than from extra weak-view queries alone.

A public-only protocol is respected throughout: quantile models, proxy auditors, reference models, and transform screening use only public or external data, and the private audit pool is never used for tuning.
The epsilon-tuned and AUC-tuned lambda choices either agree or produce materially similar audit conclusions; otherwise the method is considered unstable.

At least one augmentation family passes the pre-registered label-preservation screen for both random and FGSM canaries; otherwise the study is reported as a negative feasibility result rather than a positive method claim.
